source.title: Cognitive underpinnings of pretend play in autism
source.venue: Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders

confidence_rationale: Compared pretend play in autism (N=28), other developmental disorders (N=24), and typical children (N=26). Key finding: children with autism showed significant deficits in ToM but not EF measures, and ToM (not EF) best predicted pretend play deficits; generativity (one EF measure) showed a secondary role. This supports MECH-198's implication: pretend play failure maps primarily onto the hypothesis tag / metarepresentational decoupling system (ToM-associated), not onto general executive function. This gives REE the ability to distinguish two mechanistically distinct failure modes: ToM-deficit (missing hypothesis tag co-operation) vs. EF-deficit (frame maintenance cost). The autism model also grounds MECH-200/201 failure signatures in developmental deficit data.
failure_signatures: Children with autism who pass EF measures still show pretend play deficits -- EF is not the gating prerequisite; ToM/metarepresentational decoupling is. This contradicts executive function accounts of pretend play development.

Even with ToM deficits, some children with autism produce simple object substitution pretense -- suggesting the play-frame tag (ARC-049, behavioral/intentional level) is partially intact while the hypothesis-tag system (MECH-094) is selectively impaired.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Pretend play deficits in autism are predicted by theory of mind deficits (not executive function deficits); generativity (an EF component related to generating novel action sequences) shows a secondary but significant role.
mapping.ree_translation: Autism maps onto selective impairment of the hypothesis tag system (MECH-094): the agent lacks the metarepresentational capacity to simultaneously maintain a primary reality-tracking representation and a decoupled synthetic representation marked as counterfactual. The partial preservation of simple object substitution (even with ToM deficit) suggests ARC-049 (behavioral frame-opening via social cues) can operate without full MECH-094 -- supporting REE's two-tag decomposition. The generativity role (generating novel action sequences within play) is consistent with REE's requirement that E3 trajectory selection must function in synthetic mode during pretend play.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Autism is a complex multi-factor developmental condition; selecting it as the model for isolated hypothesis-tag impairment risks attributing multiple confounds to a single mechanism. The ToM-EF dissociation in autism is contested in more recent literature (later studies show EF deficits when more sensitive measures are used). Sample sizes are small for three-group comparison.
mapping.source_context: N=78 total (autism N=28, developmental disorders N=24, typical N=26). Cross-sectional. Multiple pretend play and cognitive measures. Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders 2003.

confidence_components.notes: Adequate source: JADD, three-group design, multiple measures. Moderate mapping fidelity: autism as model for hypothesis-tag impairment is directionally correct but mechanistically noisy. Moderate transfer risk: autism involves multiple co-occurring impairments.
